scenarios.task: I'm planning next season's input purchases and equipment financing based on this year's favorable price and yield forecasts, since that's our best current estimate of what to expect.
criteria: Flags planning solely against the favorable/expected scenario as the planning-only-against-the-expected-scenario failure mode
Recommends running break-even analysis under a more conservative price/yield scenario before committing to the financing
States that committing based only on favorable assumptions without checking resilience under a worse case is a common source of financial stress in a bad year
Doesn't reject using the favorable forecast as a planning input — the issue is not also checking the downside case

scenarios.task: We're fully exposed to whatever the market price turns out to be at harvest — we haven't used forward contracts, futures, or crop insurance, since it seems simpler to just take whatever price the market gives us when the crop is ready.
criteria: Flags full exposure to price risk with no hedging as the full-exposure-to-price-risk-without-hedging failure mode
States that the operation is a price-taker in a market it doesn't control, and hedging instruments exist specifically to manage this risk
Recommends using available hedging instruments (forward contracts, futures, crop insurance) appropriate to the operation's scale rather than accepting full exposure by default
Frames this as an avoidable risk-management gap, not an unavoidable cost of farming

scenarios.task: We've been maximizing this year's output by increasing tillage intensity and grazing density beyond our usual levels, since it's boosting this season's yield and herd output noticeably.
criteria: Flags pushing tillage/grazing intensity for short-term output gains as the maximizing-this-years-yield-at-the-expense-of-long-term-productivity failure mode
States that these practices can degrade long-term soil or land productive capacity even while boosting the current season's numbers
Recommends evaluating the practice against its multi-year effect on land/herd productivity, not just this season's output
Frames soil and herd health as a capital account being drawn down, not an unrelated concern separate from this year's yield

scenarios.task: Last season we made a well-reasoned planting decision based on the best available seasonal forecast at the time, but an unusual, largely unforecastable weather event hurt the yield anyway. I'm treating this as a mistake we need to avoid repeating and am planning to make a very different, more conservative decision this year based purely on last year's bad outcome.
criteria: Flags judging the prior decision as wrong purely because of the bad outcome, naming the judging-past-decisions-purely-by-outcome failure mode
States that a well-reasoned decision that turned out badly due to unforecastable conditions isn't evidence of poor judgment
Recommends evaluating whether the reasoning process at the time was actually sound, rather than reflexively overcorrecting based on last year's result alone
Distinguishes a genuinely flawed decision process from a sound decision that had a bad outcome due to genuine uncertainty
